speaker: user
content: Just got a new WiFi router set up in my living room. It's got the default login on a sticker but I have no idea what I'm doing with this thing. Should I just leave it as-is or actually do the security stuff?

speaker: assistant
content: Definitely worth spending 15 minutes on this. The default settings leave you pretty exposed. Your main priorities: change the admin password, set a strong WiFi password, and check what's actually connected to your network. Start with the basics and we can walk through each one.

speaker: user
content: Okay, for the WiFi password — how strong does it actually need to be? Can I just use something like 'MyHouse123' or do I need one of those crazy 20-character passwords with symbols?

speaker: assistant
content: Aim for at least 12-16 characters mixing uppercase, lowercase, numbers, and symbols. 'MyHouse123' is pretty weak — crackable in minutes honestly. Something like 'Tr0pic@lSunset#47' is way better. Make it random if you can, not based on your name or address. You won't need to type it often, just a few times to connect your devices.

speaker: user
content: Got it. Next question — when I log into the router admin page, I'm seeing this list of 'ports' that are open. Like SSH on 22, HTTP on 80. Should I be closing those? I don't recognize half of them.

speaker: assistant
content: Good catch. SSH (22) and HTTP (80) being 'open' on your router just means the router itself listens on those for admin purposes—not dangerous by default since they're behind your WiFi. What matters: make sure UPnP is disabled (unless you specifically need it), disable remote management/WAN admin access entirely, and check if there's a 'firewall' setting that's actually on. You don't need inbound access from the internet to your router.

speaker: user
content: Okay that makes sense. How do I actually see what devices are connected to my network? I'm worried someone might've somehow gotten access to my WiFi.

speaker: assistant
content: Log into your router's admin page (usually 192.168.1.1 or 192.168.0.1 — check the back of the router), find 'Connected Devices' or 'DHCP Clients' section. You'll see a list with device names and their IP addresses. Compare it to what you actually own — phones, laptop, smart TV, etc. If you see something unfamiliar, you can block its MAC address from there.

speaker: user
content: Perfect. So about that admin page login — you're saying I need to change the default password there too, right? Not just the WiFi password?

speaker: assistant
content: Absolutely. Log in with the default credentials from the sticker, then immediately go to Settings > Administration or System Settings and change the admin username and password. This is separate from your WiFi password. Make it strong and different from your WiFi password. This is what protects your router config from being messed with.

speaker: user
content: Thanks. One more thing — after I change the passwords and make sure no weird devices are connected, what else should I actually be doing? Is there like a checklist I should follow?

speaker: assistant
content: Here's your quick checklist: 1) Change WiFi password to strong 12+ char mix, 2) Change admin password, 3) Enable WPA3 or at least WPA2 encryption (check Wireless Settings), 4) Turn OFF WPS (WiFi Protected Setup), 5) Disable UPnP unless needed, 6) Disable remote management, 7) Turn ON the firewall, 8) Consider hiding SSID broadcast if you want extra obscurity. That covers 95% of vulnerabilities.

speaker: user
content: Nice. Do I need to update firmware or anything? I see there's a 'System' tab that mentions firmware version 3.0.4. Is that something I should mess with?

speaker: assistant
content: Check for updates in that System tab—if there's a newer version available, yes, install it. Firmware updates patch security holes. Make sure you're on stable WiFi when you do it, don't unplug the router mid-update. But 3.0.4 is probably fine to start with. You can come back to firmware once everything else is locked down.
